one_line_takeaway: A TMLR systematization of knowledge that reframes LLM red-teaming attacks by the level of system access an adversary needs (application input -> API -> in-context data -> model internals -> training process), tying each to CIAP adversary goals to give practitioners a threat-model-grounded map of attack surfaces and defenses.
key_finding: The main deliverable is the access-level threat model itself and the argument that organizing attacks by required system access (rather than by risk type or attack method) gives a more complete, practitioner-usable picture of adversary capabilities and where to concentrate defenses. Synthesized insights include: (1) manual/human attacks yield more diverse harmful behaviors while automated attacks cluster tightly, so humans remain essential to red-teaming; (2) larger RLHF-aligned models are considerably harder to attack than plain LMs or H3-prompted models, and H3 prompting is not reliably an effective defense (attributed to surveyed Ganguli et al. 2022b); (3) many 'safety' benchmarks correlate with model capability, so capability gains can be mistaken for safety gains; (4) transparent-access training-time attacks (backdoors, poisoning) are the most powerful and hardest to remove but require insider or state-level actors. The authors also draw a practitioner distinction between direct prompt injection (DPI, application input) and indirect prompt injection (IPI, in-context data), placing only IPI under their 'Infusion Attack' category.
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) paper (Bloomberg / NJIT / Harvard, published in TMLR 05/2025) whose OWN contribution is an organizing framework for LLM red-teaming, not any new attack or experiment. Three stated contributions: (1) a threat model structured around entry points in the LLM development-and-deployment lifecycle, so attacks and defenses can be reasoned about by the level of system access an adversary needs; (2) a taxonomy of attacks built on that threat model, plus a survey of common defense methodologies; and (3) a synthesis of insights from prior work about what makes red-teaming effective. The novel framing is organizing attacks by required access level (five 'boxes': Application Input, LLM API, In-Context Data, Model Internals, Training Process) rather than by risk category (hate/misinfo/privacy) or by method (manual/automatic), which the authors argue aligns better with ML practitioners' mental model and links each attack back to a concrete adversary capability. Nearly all specific attacks, datasets, and numbers in the paper are drawn from surveyed prior work, not produced by the authors.
threat_model: The paper explicitly builds a threat model (Section 3). Attack surface is decomposed into five access-ordered entry points (Fig. 2, Table 2): Box 1 Application Input (non-programmatic prompt crafting, widest surface, anyone), Box 2 LLM API (programmatic access to temperature/top-k/logit_bias, side channels, model-probability extraction), Box 3 In-Context Data (poison RAG documents, search indices, ICL examples, external tools), Box 4 Model Internals (modify activations, embedding space, decoding, tokenizer), Box 5 Training Process (poison training data, alter algorithm, insert backdoors, erode alignment via fine-tuning). Adversary types are ranked by capability/resources: internal actor (red-team member / competition participant), weak eavesdropper (hobbyist, hacker, user), and strong state-level actor (e.g., intelligence agency) for web-scale poisoning or weight extraction. Adversary goals are framed through the CIAP lens (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Privacy, following Papernot et al. 2018): e.g., jailbreaks target Integrity; inversion/side-channel attacks target Confidentiality/Privacy/Availability (Table 3).
limitations: As an SoK, it contributes organization and synthesis rather than new empirical evidence; its numbers and attack efficacy claims are inherited from cited works and not independently validated here. Scope is explicitly bounded (Section 2.4): it excludes programming-language/cybersecurity exploits, covert malware in AI dev platforms, watermark-evasion attacks, and vision-language-model attacks (though it does touch MLLM activation attacks like AnyDoor). The taxonomy's access-based boundaries are admittedly loose at sublevels (e.g., GCG/AutoDAN could sit in either the transferable black-box or transparent training-attack bucket depending on whether run on a proxy or the target model). The defense survey is high-level ('key papers') rather than a systematic effectiveness comparison, and the authors concede red-teaming 'is not a panacea' and must complement other governance. Rapid field churn means the catalog is a snapshot.

datasets_used: No dataset of the authors' own (survey/SoK). Notable datasets it catalogs from surveyed work: Shen et al. 2023 in-the-wild jailbreak set of 6,847 prompts from 4 platforms (Nov 2022-May 2023); HackAPrompt competition corpus of 600,000+ adversarial prompts (Schulhoff et al. 2023); Ganguli et al. 2022b released dataset of 38,961 red-team attacks used to study scaling

Risk-taxonomy references compiled in Table 1: Llama Guard, Decoding Trust, OpenAI Moderation categories, MLCommons AI Safety hazard categories, ALERT, Perspective API, AVID Taxonomy Matrix, AEGIS, AI Risk Categorization
quantitative_results: Zhang et al. 2024d (cited): poisoning just 0.1% of pre-training data suffices for attacks to persist through post-training alignment
Rando & Tramer 2023 (cited): ~5% of RLHF training data corruption embeds a universal-trigger backdoor surviving reward-model + fine-tuning phases on a 13B model
Wan et al. 2023 (cited): as few as 100 poisoned samples induce a targeted behavior in instruction tuning
Qi et al. 2023 (cited): safety mechanisms compromised with as few as 10-100 adversarial fine-tuning examples; even benign fine-tuning erodes safety
Zhan et al. 2023 (cited): 95% success circumventing GPT-4 RLHF safeguards with only 340 examples
Lermen et al. 2023 (cited): LoRA fine-tuning drops Llama refusal rate to below 1% on refusal benchmarks
Bhardwaj & Poria 2023 (cited): 88% harmful-response rate for ChatGPT and 91% for Vicuna-7B / LLaMA-2-Chat with 100 samples
Shen et al. 2023 (cited): 6,847 in-the-wild jailbreak prompts analyzed; Schulhoff et al. 2023 HackAPrompt collected 600,000+ adversarial prompts vs GPT-3, ChatGPT, FlanT5-XXL across 10 challenges
Arditi et al. 2024 (cited): refusal behavior traced to a one-dimensional latent subspace across 13 open-source chat models
Ganguli et al. 2022b (cited): 38,961-attack scaling study showing RLHF-aligned models are harder to attack; H3 prompting not consistently effective
baselines_compared: None in an experimental sense; this is a survey with no head-to-head evaluation of its own. It instead qualitatively contrasts its access-based taxonomy against prior organizing schemes: risk-type taxonomies (Weidinger et al. 2021; Wang et al. 2023 DecodingTrust), method-based taxonomies (Inie et al. 2023; Schulhoff et al. 2023 prompt-only; Geiping et al. 2024; Dong et al. 2024b training/inference split; Chowdhury et al. 2024 three-technique split; MITRE ATLAS 14 strategies; Shayegani et al. 2023 input-modality), and identifies Feffer et al. 2024's four-category scheme (brute-force, brute-force+AI, algorithmic search, targeted) as the closest prior work
